practice_questions.question_text: A 53-year-old male without a significant past medical history presents to the emergency department for evaluation of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Pertinent findings on presentation include notable tenderness in the epigastrium and a lipase level >3 times the upper limit of normal. He is admitted for acute pancreatitis. Intravenous lactated Ringer solution, antiemetics, and pain control are initiated. At the time of your evaluation he has normal vital signs and reports improved symptoms, although you still note abdominal tenderness. When entering admission orders for this patient, which one of the following diets would be most appropriate?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Full liquids"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Low-carbohydrate"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Low-fat"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "NPO"}]
practice_questions.explanation: Pancreatitis is associated with an intense inflammatory response that increases caloric needs, and nutrition management is a key component of care. Acute pancreatitis is also associated with reduced perfusion of the intestinal mucosa, leading to tissue damage and increased intestinal permeability. Enteral feeding provides calories and increases splanchnic blood flow, leading to improved integrity of the bowel wall and improved gut motility. Patients with acute pancreatitis benefit from early enteral feeding within 24 hours of admission. A Cochrane review found that enteral nutrition has been associated with lower mortality, less multiple organ failure, and less systemic infection compared with parenteral nutrition. In severe acute pancreatitis, the benefits of enteral nutrition appear to be even more notable with a relative risk of death of 0.18 (95% CI 0.06 to 0.58). Liquid and soft diets have no evidence of benefit over a low-fat solid diet, and some studies found a shorter length of stay in patients started on solid diets rather than liquid diets. If patients cannot tolerate an oral diet within 72 hours, enteral nutrition should be provided via nasogastric or nasojejunal tube. Parenteral nutrition should be administered in patients who cannot tolerate tube feeding. Low-carbohydrate diets are not recommended because they are often high in fat and may worsen pancreatic inflammation.
practice_questions.reference: Ellis R, Ellis C. Dog and cat bites. Am Fam Physician. 2014;90(4):239-243. | Ortiz DD, Lezcano FO. Dog and cat bites: rapid evidence review. Am Fam Physician. 2023;108(5):501-505.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["acute pancreatitis"], "drugs": ["lactated Ringer solution", "antiemetics"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["lipase >3 times upper limit of normal"], "concept_summary": "Acute pancreatitis patients benefit from early enteral nutrition with a low-fat diet rather than NPO status to maintain gut integrity and provide needed calories."}

practice_questions.question_text: According to guidelines from the American Gastroenterological Association, which one of the following is recommended as the best pharmacologic treatment of typical symptoms in diarrhea-predominant irritable bowel syndrome?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Amitriptyline"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Dicyclomine"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Linaclotide (Linzess)"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Lubiprostone (Amitiza)"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Rifaximin (Xifaxan)"}]
practice_questions.explanation: According to the 2022 American Gastroenterological Association guidelines for diarrhea- predominant irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-D), the nonabsorbable antibiotic rifaximin has the best evidence for controlling diarrhea, discomfort, bloating, and abdominal pain. It has a broad spectrum with activity against gram-positive, gram-negative (including Clostridioides difficile), and anaerobic bacteria. It is thought to work by reducing bacterial byproducts and altering the gut flora. It is prescribed as a 14-day course for acute and recurrent symptoms. Rifaximin does not have any significant adverse effects, but it is a high-cost medication. Low-dose amitriptyline and dicyclomine are often effective in reducing symptoms, but the evidence is not as strong as that of rifaximin. Linaclotide is used for constipation- predominant IBS (IBS-C), but its chief side effect of diarrhea results in many patients discontinuing the medication. Lubiprostone is another medication used for IBS-C and for opioid-induced constipation, not IBS-D.
practice_questions.reference: Karuppiah S, Pomianowski K. Rifaximin (Xifaxan) for irritable bowel syndrome. Am Fam Physician. 2017;95(4):258-259. | Chang L, Sultan S, Lembo A, Verne GN, Smalley W, Heidelbaugh JJ. AGA clinical practice guideline on the pharmacologic management of irritable bowel syndrome with constipation. Gastroenterology. 2022;163(1):118-136. | Arnold MJ. Medications for irritable bowel syndrome: guidelines from the AGA. Am Fam Physician. 2023;108(5):527-529. | Lembo A, Sultan S, Chang L, Heidelbaugh JJ, Smalley W, Verne GN. AGA clinical practice guideline on the pharmacological management of irritable bowel syndrome with diarrhea. Gastroenterology. 2022;163(1)137-151.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["diarrhea-predominant irritable bowel syndrome", "IBS-D"], "drugs": ["rifaximin", "amitriptyline", "dicyclomine", "linaclotide", "lubiprostone"], "guidelines": ["AGA 2022"], "thresholds": [], "concept_summary": "Tests knowledge that rifaximin is the best evidence-based pharmacologic treatment for diarrhea-predominant IBS according to AGA guidelines."}

practice_questions.question_text: An otherwise healthy 26-year-old female presents to your clinic with a history of 3 episodes of vertigo occurring over the last week. Each episode lasts for several hours. During the episodes, she notes fullness in the right ear with tinnitus and hearing loss. She has not noticed anything specific that triggers the episodes, and reports consuming 1 glass of wine per night. She does not report headache, visual changes, fever, or upper respiratory symptoms. She is currently asymptomatic and a physical examination is unremarkable. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Recommending a reduction in alcohol intake"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Performing the Epley maneuver"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Ordering audiometry"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Initiating oral hydrochlorothiazide, 25 mg daily"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Initiating oral prednisone, 20 mg daily for 5 days"}]
practice_questions.explanation: Meniere disease is characterized by spontaneous recurring episodes of vertigo with associated unilateral hearing loss, tinnitus, and nystagmus. Even though there are no specific diagnostic tests, audiometry should be performed in patients suspected of having this condition. Sensorineural hearing loss may or may not be present on audiometric evaluation. Acute attacks should be treated with medication such as promethazine, diazepam, or prochlorperazine to suppress vestibular function. Lifestyle interventions, including salt restriction and reduction in alcohol and caffeine intake, have traditionally been recommended. However, there is no sufficient evidence to support these recommendations. The Epley maneuver is a repositioning treatment used for benign paroxysmal positional vertigo and not Meniere disease. There is no good-quality evidence that diuretics, antivirals, or corticosteroids treat or prevent Meniere disease.
practice_questions.reference: Basura GJ, Adams ME, Monfared A, et al. Clinical practice guideline: Meniere's Disease Executive Summary. Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg. 2020;162(4):415-434. | Webster KE, Galbraith K, Harrington-Benton NA, et al. Systemic pharmacological interventions for Meniere's disease. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2023;2(2):CD015171. | Rogers TS, Noel MA, Garcia B. Dizziness: evaluation and management. Am Fam Physician. 2023;107(5):514-523. | Webster KE, George B, Lee A, et al. Lifestyle and dietary interventions for Meniere's disease. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2023;(2):CD015244.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["Meniere disease"], "drugs": ["promethazine", "diazepam", "prochlorperazine"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": [], "concept_summary": "This question tests diagnosis of Meniere disease through its classic triad of episodic vertigo, unilateral hearing loss, and tinnitus, with audiometry being the appropriate initial diagnostic test."}

practice_questions.question_text: A 75-year-old female presents to your office with a 1-year history of gradually worsening right-sided groin pain on standing. The pain improves with rest. These symptoms have been marginally relieved with ibuprofen and acetaminophen, but her use of these medications has been limited by gastric irritation. A physical examination is most notable for pain in the groin with passive flexion, adduction, and internal rotation (FADIR) testing. Which one of the following treatments would be most helpful in relieving her symptoms?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "A topical lidocaine patch (Lidoderm)"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Hydrocodone"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Tramadol"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Intra-articular corticosteroid injection"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Intra-articular hyaluronic acid injection"}]
practice_questions.explanation: This patient presents with hip osteoarthritis. Intra-articular corticosteroid injections have been shown to relieve hip and osteoarthritis pain for at least 4 months. In addition to pain relief, these injections may improve activity levels and quality of life. In contrast to intra- articular corticosteroid injections, intra-articular hyaluronic acid injections have not been shown to be substantially beneficial in relieving symptoms of hip osteoarthritis. Topical lidocaine patches have not been shown to be effective for management of pain due to hip osteoarthritis and are not a sustainable solution for this patient. Opioids such as hydrocodone are advised against for treatment of chronic osteoarthritis. They are not effective over the long term, have significant side effects, and carry a high risk of addiction and misuse. Tramadol is not effective for pain relief or functional improvement in hip osteoarthritis and carries similar risks as opioids.
practice_questions.reference: Arnold MJ. Hip osteoarthritis: guidelines from the American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons. Am Fam Physician. 2025;111(1):90-91. | Nandi S, Hannon CP, Fillingham Y. 2023 American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons management of osteoarthritis of the hip evidence-based clinical practice guideline: case studies. J Am Acad Orthop Surg. 2025;33(4):e220-e223. | Alkaramany E, Murshid A, Alhaneedi GA. Appropriateness of hip osteoarthritis management in clinical practice compared with the American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) criteria. Int Orthop. 2021;45(11):2805-2810.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["hip osteoarthritis"], "drugs": ["lidocaine", "hydrocodone", "tramadol", "corticosteroid injection", "hyaluronic acid injection"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": [], "concept_summary": "Tests treatment options for hip osteoarthritis when oral medications are not tolerated, specifically that intra-articular corticosteroid injection provides effective pain relief for several months."}

practice_questions.question_text: A 17-year-old female with well-controlled diabetes mellitus is brought to your office by her mother for a sports preparticipation physical evaluation prior to playing soccer. She has intermittent knee pain when running but no other symptoms. Which one of the following, if present in this patient, would be a CONTRAINDICATION to playing soccer?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "A BMI >30 kg/m2"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "An enlarged spleen"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Insulin-dependent diabetes"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Mitral valve prolapse"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Tibial tubercle apophysitis"}]
practice_questions.explanation: A sports preparticipation evaluation is important for assessing health risks and ensuring an athlete is not unnecessarily restricted from play. An athlete with an acutely enlarged spleen or liver faces an increased risk of organ rupture during contact sports. This patient should be evaluated for a cause of the organomegaly, if present, prior to participation. Obesity in an athlete increases the risk for heat-related illness, but it is not an indication to restrict play. Exercise is beneficial for adolescents with diabetes mellitus and may require modification of insulin and eating regimens. Asymptomatic mild mitral valve prolapse is not a reason to deny participation. Tibial tubercle apophysitis is not a contraindication to play, and most experts encourage activity and conservative management.
practice_questions.reference: Rice SG; American Academy of Pediatrics Council on Sports Medicine and Fitness. Medical conditions affecting sports participation. Pediatrics. 2008;121(4):841-848. | Bernhardt DT, Roberts WO, eds; American Academy of Family Physicians. PPE: Preparticipation Physical Evaluation. 5th ed. American Academy of Pediatrics; 2019.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["splenomegaly", "organomegaly"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["BMI >30 kg/m2"], "concept_summary": "This question tests sports participation contraindications, specifically that enlarged spleen poses significant risk of organ rupture during contact sports and is an absolute contraindication to participation."}

practice_questions.question_text: A generally healthy 56-year-old female sees you for follow-up of pneumonia diagnosed in the emergency department (ED). A chest x-ray performed in the ED showed a left lower lobe infiltrate and several compression fractures involving the thoracic vertebrae. A DXA scan performed 7 months ago showed a T-score of ”“1.9. Her daily medications include a multivitamin; calcium, 1000 mg; and vitamin D (cholecalciferol), 800 IU. Today she feels 3 well and has no concerns. This patient would be most likely to benefit from therapy for which one of the following conditions?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Osteopenia"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Osteoporosis"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Spondylolisthesis"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Spondylolysis"}]
practice_questions.explanation: Although this patient's T-score indicates osteopenia, the presence of fragility fractures indicates osteoporosis. The T-score is obtained by measuring bone mineral density using DXA at the spine, total hip, or femoral neck. A T-score between ”“1.0 and ”“2.49 indicates osteopenia, whereas a T-score of â‰¤”“2.5 denotes osteoporosis. However, postmenopausal osteoporosis may be diagnosed by bone mineral density or by the presence of a fragility fracture, which is a fracture that occurs with no associated trauma or after a fall from standing height or lower. Hence, osteoporosis can be diagnosed and the patient further evaluated and treated even though her T-score is >”“2.5. Spondylolisthesis is the acquired anterior displacement of one vertebral body over another and is typically associated with degenerative changes. Spondylolysis is a cause of acute back pain resulting from a stress fracture involving the pars interarticularis of the lumbar vertebral body. It is typically found in young athletes.
practice_questions.reference: NASS Evidence-Based Clinical Guidelines Committee; Matz P, Meagher RJ, Lamer T, et al. | Evidence-Based Clinical Guidelines for Multidisciplinary Spine Care: Diagnosis and Treatment of Degenerative Lumbar Spondylolisthesis. 2nd ed. North American Spine Society; 2014. | Walker MD, Shane E. Postmenopausal osteoporosis. N Engl J Med. 2023;389(21):1979�1991.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["osteoporosis", "osteopenia", "fragility fractures"], "drugs": ["calcium", "vitamin D"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["T-score -1.9", "T-score -1.0 to -2.49", "T-score \u2264-2.5"], "concept_summary": "Tests understanding that fragility fractures indicate osteoporosis regardless of T-score, overriding the osteopenia classification from DXA alone."}

practice_questions.question_text: A 27-year-old patient presents to the emergency department with a fever, headache, and stiff neck. You diagnose sepsis and perform a lumbar puncture. The results are consistent with bacterial meningitis. In addition to antibiotics, which one of the following intravenous therapies is most likely to improve mortality?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Acyclovir"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Dexamethasone"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Furosemide"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Glycerol"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Immunoglobulin"}]
practice_questions.explanation: Despite optimal therapy, the mortality and loss of quality-adjusted life-years from bacterial meningitis is significant. Empiric therapy for adults includes vancomycin and ceftriaxone. Intravenous (IV) dexamethasone, when given shortly before or at the time of administration of IV antibiotics, has been shown to improve mortality (odds ratio 0.90) and neurologic sequelae. Bactericidal antibiotics help to reduce inflammation. Acyclovir would be indicated if there is clinical concern for herpes simplex virus or symptoms of encephalitis. While optimal fluid management for meningitis should be tailored based on clinical evaluation, this patient has also been diagnosed with sepsis and should receive appropriate fluid resuscitation; therefore, IV furosemide would be inappropriate. Glycerol is a hyperosmolar agent intended to reduce intracranial pressure in bacterial meningitis, but it cannot be recommended for treatment due to an increased risk of death according to available data in adults with a high prevalence of HIV. IV immunoglobulin is not a recognized treatment for bacterial meningitis.
practice_questions.reference: Hasbun R. Progress and challenges in bacterial meningitis: a review. JAMA. 2022;328(21):2147-2154. | Mount HR, Boyle SD. Aseptic and bacterial meningitis: evaluation, treatment, and prevention. Am Fam Physician. 2017;96(5):314-322. | Ajdukiewicz KM, Cartwright KE, Scarborough M, et al. Glycerol adjuvant therapy in adults with bacterial meningitis in a high HIV seroprevalence setting in Malawi: a double-blind, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Infect Dis. 2011;11(4):293-300. | Brouwer MC, McIntyre P, Prasad K, van de Beek D. Corticosteroids for acute bacterial meningitis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015;2015(9):CD004405.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["bacterial meningitis", "sepsis"], "drugs": ["dexamethasone", "vancomycin", "ceftriaxone", "acyclovir"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["odds ratio 0.90"], "concept_summary": "Tests knowledge that IV dexamethasone given with or before antibiotics improves mortality and neurologic outcomes in bacterial meningitis."}

practice_questions.question_text: A 65-year-old male presents to your clinic with symptoms of flashing lights and increased floaters in his left eye. He does not have any pain, and he has no past medical history. His vital signs are normal. His visual acuity is at baseline when wearing his glasses, and you note a visual field defect on the left. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Migraine with aura"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Central retinal artery occlusion"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Retinal detachment"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Globe rupture"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Acute ischemic stroke"}]
practice_questions.explanation: Family physicians frequently evaluate ophthalmologic symptoms and should be familiar with conditions requiring emergent evaluation. Peripheral flashing lights and floaters are common symptoms of retinal detachment. This patient should be referred for urgent ophthalmologic evaluation including a dilated fundoscopic examination. While positive and negative visual features are seen with migraine with aura, these features should be transient and followed by a headache typical of migraine. New-onset migraine in a 65-year-old is also uncommon and would require further evaluation. Central retinal artery occlusion, often ischemic or thromboembolic, typically occurs in those with risk factors such as diabetes mellitus, vascular disease, and tobacco use. Rapid, painless vision loss is often accompanied by afferent pupillary defect and the lesion can be visualized on a dilated fundoscopic examination as a cherry-red spot. Globe rupture is unlikely to occur without a history of trauma. Acute ischemic stroke may cause vision loss but is unlikely to cause ocular symptoms of flashing lights and floaters.
practice_questions.reference: Gelston CD, Deitz GA. Eye emergencies. Am Fam Physician. 2020;102(9):539-545. | Raharja A, Whitefield L. Clinical approach to vision loss: a review for general physicians. Clin Med (Lond). 2022;22(2):95-99. | Bagheri N, Mehta S. Acute vision loss. Prim Care. 2015;42(3):347-361.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["retinal detachment", "migraine with aura", "central retinal artery occlusion", "globe rupture", "acute ischemic stroke"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": [], "concept_summary": "Testing ability to recognize retinal detachment presenting with flashing lights, floaters, and visual field defects in a patient requiring urgent ophthalmologic referral."}

practice_questions.question_text: A 65-year-old male presents to the emergency department with an acute onset of bloody urine confirmed to be hematuria on urinalysis. He does not have any pain, fever, or recent trauma. His medical history is significant for hypertension that is managed with lisinopril (Zestril). A physical examination is unremarkable. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Repeating the urinalysis in 1 month"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Initiating empiric antibiotic therapy and re-evaluating in 1 week"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Ordering urine cytology"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Referring the patient for immediate cystoscopy and imaging studies"}]
practice_questions.correct_text: Referring the patient for immediate cystoscopy and imaging studies
practice_questions.explanation: The American College of Physicians recommends that all adults with gross hematuria be referred for further urologic evaluation, including cystoscopy and imaging, due to the high association with malignancy. The sensitivity and specificity of cystoscopy for detecting bladder cancer are high, with a sensitivity ranging from 87% to 100% and a specificity of 64% to 100%. The positive predictive value of gross hematuria for urologic malignancy in older men is high. Gross hematuria warrants immediate investigation to rule out serious underlying conditions; thus a repeat urinalysis in 1 month would not be appropriate. Initiating empiric antibiotic therapy and re-evaluating in 1 week would not be appropriate without evidence of infection. Urine cytology has limited sensitivity and specificity and should not replace cystoscopy and imaging for the initial evaluation of hematuria.
practice_questions.reference: Nielsen M, Qaseem A; High Value Care Task Force of the American College of Physicians. Hematuria as a marker of occult urinary tract cancer: advice for high-value care from the American College of Physicians. Ann Intern Med. 2016;164(7):488-497. | Wolfman DJ, Marko J, Nikolaidis P, et al; Expert Panel on Urological Imaging. ACR Appropriateness Criteria hematuria. J Am Coll Radiol. 2020;17(suppl 5):S138-S147. | Devlies W, de Jong JJ, Hofmann F, et al. The diagnostic accuracy of cystoscopy for detecting bladder cancer in adults presenting with haematuria: a systematic review from the European Association of Urology Guidelines Office. Eur Urol Focus. 2024;10(1):115�122.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["gross hematuria", "bladder cancer", "urologic malignancy"], "drugs": ["lisinopril"], "guidelines": ["American College of Physicians"], "thresholds": ["sensitivity 87-100%", "specificity 64-100%"], "concept_summary": "Testing knowledge that all adults with gross hematuria require immediate urologic evaluation with cystoscopy and imaging due to high malignancy risk."}

practice_questions.question_text:
A 30-year-old soccer player presents with a hyperextension injury of his right wrist after falling and 
landing on his outstretched hand. On examination the wrist is swollen and tender over the anatomic 
snuffbox. A plain radiograph shows soft tissue swelling. 
 
In addition to ice and elevation, which one of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Gutter splint"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Thumb spica splint"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Volar splint"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Short arm cast"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Long arm cast"}]
practice_questions.explanation:
The patient should be presumed to have a scaphoid fracture and treated with a thumb 
spica splint for up to 8 weeks. An initial plain radiograph may be normal in up to 20% 
of scaphoid fractures. Radiography can be repeated in 2 weeks if the wrist remains 
tender. A gutter splint, volar splint, short arm cast, and long arm cast would not be 
appropriate for treatment of a scaphoid fracture.  
 
Reference 
Eiff MP, Hatch R. Fracture Management for Primary Care. 3rd ed. Elsevier Saunders; 
2018:84-90.
practice_questions.reference: Eiff MP, Hatch R: Fracture Management for Primary Care, ed 3 updated. Elsevier Saunders, 2018, pp 201-203.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["scaphoid fracture"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["20% normal initial radiographs", "8 weeks treatment", "2 weeks repeat imaging"], "concept_summary": "Tests recognition of scaphoid fracture based on anatomic snuffbox tenderness and mechanism, requiring thumb spica splinting even with normal initial radiographs."}

practice_questions.question_text:
A 17-year-old male sustains an injury while playing in a high school football game. As the sideline 
physician, you determine that he needs to be transported to the emergency department with neck 
stabilization. He is awake, talking, and in no acute distress. There is no concern for airway 
compromise. 
 
Depending on the nature of his other injuries, which one of the following pieces of protective 
equipment should you remove before transporting the patient?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Only the helmet if required"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Only the shoulder pads if required"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Both the helmet and the shoulder pads"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Neither the helmet nor the shoulder pads"}]
practice_questions.correct_text: Neither the helmet nor the shoulder pads
practice_questions.explanation:
In general, helmets and pads should remain in place during transportation after a 
football injury requiring neck stabilization. The facemask may be removed with a special 
tool if there are airway issues or loss of consciousness. If the athlete’s injuries require 
either the helmet or pads to be removed, both should be removed by a trained team. 
Removal of only one can cause hyperflexion or hyperextension of the neck.  
 
References  
Mills BM, Conrick KM, Anderson S, et al. Consensus recommendations on the 
prehospital care of the injured athlete with a suspected catastrophic cervical spine 
injury. J Athl Train. 2020;55(6):563-572.   
 
Usman S. Management of head and neck injuries by the sideline physician. Am Fam 
Physician. 2022;106(5):543-548.
practice_questions.reference: Usman S. Management of head and neck injuries by the sideline physician. Am Fam Physician. 2022;106(5):543-548.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["cervical spine injury", "football injury"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": [], "concept_summary": "Tests knowledge of proper helmet and shoulder pad removal protocols during spinal immobilization after football injuries - both must be removed together by trained personnel to prevent neck hyperflexion/hyperextension."}

practice_questions.question_text:
A 72-year-old female presents with pain in her right foot after missing a step while descending a 
staircase. She was leading with her right foot when she stumbled. On examination she has tenderness 
in the right dorsal and plantar midfoot. Foot x-rays are normal. An image of the right plantar foot is 
shown below. 
Which one of the following would be most appropriate at this time?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Non\u201d\u201cweight bearing status, plus CT or MRI"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Corticosteroid injection"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Heel splints and physical therapy"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Rigid ankle support and physical therapy"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Rigid ankle support and referral to an orthopedic surgeon"}]
practice_questions.correct_text: Non”“weight bearing status, plus CT or MRI
practice_questions.explanation:
This patient has a Lisfranc injury of the right midfoot. Lisfranc injuries occur to the 
tarsometatarsal joint complex of the foot, which is stabilized by a complex ligamentous 
structure. Injuries can range from simple subluxations to complex fractures with 
dislocations. Falling forward on a plantar-flexed foot, such as when missing a stair step, 
is a common mechanism of injury. Forced dorsiflexion, which often occurs in sports or 
motor vehicle collisions, is another common mechanism. Missed Lisfranc injuries can 
lead to instability, deformity, and painful chronic osteoarthritis of the midfoot.  
X-rays are the first step in diagnosis but have a sensitivity of around 84%. Plantar 
bruising is a classic sign and should trigger a high index of suspicion for Lisfranc injury 
even with negative plain or weight-bearing x-rays, and CT or MRI should be ordered. If 
a Lisfranc injury is seen on imaging or is highly suspected, the patient should remain 
non”“weight bearing. Depending on the severity of the injury, referral to an orthopedic 
surgeon may be warranted after definitive imaging. Corticosteroid injection, heel splints, 
rigid ankle support, and physical therapy are not appropriate at this time.  
 
References  
Grewal US, Onubogu K, Southgate C, Dhinsa BS. Lisfranc injury: a review and simplified 
treatment algorithm. Foot (Edinb). 2020;45:101719.    
 
Silver S, Williams E, Plunkett ML. Common foot fractures. Am Fam Physician. 
2024;109(2):119-129.
practice_questions.reference: Grewal US, Onubogu K, Southgate C, Dhinsa BS. Lisfranc injury: a review and simplified treatment algorithm. Foot (Edinb). 2020;45:101719. | Silver S, Williams E, Plunkett ML. Common foot fractures. Am Fam Physician. 2024;109(2):119-129.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["Lisfranc injury", "tarsometatarsal joint injury"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": [], "concept_summary": "Tests recognition of Lisfranc injury mechanism (plantar-flexed foot fall) and management requiring non-weight bearing status plus advanced imaging (CT/MRI) due to high risk of missed diagnosis on plain radiographs."}

practice_questions.question_text: A 35-year-old patient presents with a 14-day history of a productive cough and green sputum. They report they initially had a fever but that it resolved after a few days. The cough is interfering with their sleep and daily activity, and they have tried cough drops without relief. They have a history of hypothyroidism for which they take levothyroxine (Synthroid), and do not have a history of asthma. They do not smoke, and their immunizations are up to date. On examination, the patient is afebrile and has an oxygen saturation of 98% on room air, a heart rate of 82 beats/min, and a blood pressure of 120/82 mm Hg. A lung examination reveals mild rhonchi that clear with cough, and you do not note any wheezing or egophony. Which one of the following treatments would be the most effective in reducing this patient's cough?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "An albuterol inhaler (Proventil, Ventolin)"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Amoxicillin/clavulanate (Augmentin)"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Azithromycin (Zithromax)"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Codeine/guaifenesin syrup"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Dextromethorphan (Delsym)"}]
practice_questions.explanation: Acute bronchitis is a lower respiratory tract infection that is most commonly caused by a virus. Treatment is generally supportive as it is a self-limited illness. Guaifenesin, honey, and dextromethorphan have been shown to decrease the frequency of cough (SOR B). In otherwise healthy patients without evidence of pneumonia, acute bronchitis can last 2”“3 weeks. Patients may exhibit purulent sputum, rhonchi, and wheezing with acute bronchitis but are typically afebrile with normal oxygen saturation. β -Agonists such as albuterol are not recommended for the treatment of acute bronchitis 2 unless wheezing is present (SOR B). While 90% of acute bronchitis cases are caused by viruses, a significant percentage are treated inappropriately with antibiotics. Antibiotics can decrease the duration of cough by 0.46 days, but the overall net benefit of antibiotics is negligible and often outweighed by potential side effects (SOR A). Studies show codeine has no benefit in the treatment of acute bronchitis and has rather significant side effects.
practice_questions.reference: Killeen BM, Wolfson AB. Antibiotics for acute bronchitis. Am Fam Physician. 2020;102(9):Online.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["acute bronchitis"], "drugs": ["dextromethorphan", "albuterol", "amoxicillin/clavulanate", "azithromycin", "codeine", "guaifenesin"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["2-3 weeks duration"], "concept_summary": "Tests appropriate management of acute bronchitis as a viral, self-limited condition requiring symptomatic treatment rather than antibiotics."}

practice_questions.question_text: A 55-year-old male presents to the urgent care clinic with a 24-hour history of worsening fever, chills, and body aches. He tells you that he discovered a tick on his scalp during a camping trip last week. He does not have a rash, headache, or palpitations, and a neurologic examination is negative. A Lyme enzyme immunoassay and confirmatory Western blot are positive. When he has taken doxycycline in the past, he has developed headaches, severe nausea, and vomiting. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate treatment option for this patient?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Amoxicillin"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Ceftriaxone"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Chloramphenicol"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Doxycycline plus promethazine"}]
practice_questions.explanation: In the United States, multiple diseases are carried by ticks including Lyme disease, Rocky Mountain spotted fever, ehrlichiosis, anaplasmosis, and Colorado tick fever. Tick-borne illnesses should be considered in patients who present with influenza-like symptoms during the spring and summer months and who have spent time outdoors. Lyme disease can be diagnosed clinically in patients with erythema migrans (SOR C). In patients such as this who present without a rash, a 2-tiered testing regimen is recommended. Doxycycline is the preferred first-line treatment, but for patients who are pregnant or do not tolerate it, an appropriate alternative is amoxicillin, 500 mg 3 times daily for 14”“21 days. It is important to prescribe a medication that the patient can tolerate for its full course, as partial treatment of the infection could lead to more serious complications. Cefuroxime axetil is another treatment option. Intravenous ceftriaxone is indicated for patients with Lyme disease who present with late neurologic manifestations, but that is not the case for this patient. Chloramphenicol is the preferred treatment for Rocky Mountain spotted fever in patients with contraindications to doxycycline therapy.
practice_questions.reference: Pace EJ, O�Reilly M. Tickborne diseases: diagnosis and management. Am Fam Physician. 2020;101(9):530-540.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["Lyme disease"], "drugs": ["amoxicillin", "ceftriaxone", "chloramphenicol", "doxycycline", "promethazine"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": [], "concept_summary": "Tests alternative antibiotic therapy for Lyme disease when doxycycline causes GI intolerance, specifically that amoxicillin is the preferred alternative for early localized or disseminated Lyme disease."}

practice_questions.question_text: A 14-year-old male sees you to establish care. He has a past medical history of asthma and uses an albuterol inhaler (Proventil, Ventolin) as needed. He normally has symptoms about 3”“4 days per week. Recent pulmonary function tests showed mildly reduced lung function. Based on the Global Initiative for Asthma (GIN
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "guidelines, which one of the following would be the preferred treatment strategy for this patient? A) Continuing as-needed albuterol"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Switching to as-needed formoterol"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Switching to an as-needed low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) plus formoterol"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Continuing as-needed albuterol, and adding a low-dose ICS for maintenance"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Continuing as-needed albuterol, and adding a low-dose ICS plus salmeterol for maintenance"}]
practice_questions.correct_text: Switching to an as-needed low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) plus formoterol
practice_questions.explanation: Based on the Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) guideline, patients who have symptoms <3”“5 days per week with normal or mildly reduced lung function are classified as having mild asthma. For patients 12 years and older with mild asthma, the guidelines recommend treatment with as-needed low-dose inhaled corticosteroid plus formoterol (ICS/formoterol). As-needed low-dose ICS/formoterol reduces the risk of severe asthma exacerbations by 60%”“64% compared to as-needed short-acting β-agonists (SABAs) such as albuterol. ICS/formoterol also reduces the risk of severe exacerbations compared to other regimens that include a SABA reliever. Formoterol should not be used without an ICS.
practice_questions.reference: Global Initiative for Asthma. Global Strategy for Asthma Management and Prevention. Updated 2022.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["mild asthma"], "drugs": ["albuterol", "formoterol", "inhaled corticosteroids", "salmeterol"], "guidelines": ["GINA"], "thresholds": ["3-4 days per week symptoms"], "concept_summary": "This question tests GINA guidelines for mild asthma management, requiring knowledge that as-needed low-dose ICS/formoterol is preferred over SABA alone for patients with symptoms 3-5 days per week."}

practice_questions.question_text:
A 23-year-old kindergarten teacher presents with a 2-day history of fever up to 102 °F, a sore throat, 
headache, and swollen glands. She has not had a runny nose or cough. Her throat is hyperemic and 
has tonsillar exudates. The anterior cervical nodes are enlarged and tender, but there is no other 
lymphadenopathy. She requests an antibiotic to prevent complications and to reduce the likelihood of 
spreading the infection to her students. A throat swab for group A Streptococcus is positive. 
 
Which one of the following is true regarding the use of antibiotics in this patient?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Her current symptoms will not be reduced"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Her risk for developing poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis will not change"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Her risk for developing rheumatic fever will not change"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "She will remain contagious for 7\u201d\u201c10 days even with treatment"}]
practice_questions.correct_text: Her risk for developing poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis will not change
practice_questions.explanation:
Up to 90% of cases of pharyngitis in adults are viral in etiology. The patient in this 
clinical scenario, however, has a high clinical likelihood of group A β-hemolytic 
streptococcal (GABS) pharyngitis, as she meets 4 of the 5 Modified Centor Criteria 
(fever, hyperemic/exudative tonsillitis, cervical adenopathy, and absence of cough). 
Antibiotic treatment is recommended to reduce communicability, relieve symptoms, and 
help prevent rheumatic fever (SOR C). Antibiotic treatment for GABS pharyngitis does 
not lower the risk for acute poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis. The use of the 
Modified Centor Criteria for acute pharyngitis is endorsed by both the American 
Academy of Family Physicians and the American College of Physicians.  
 
Reference   
Zoorob R, Sidani MA, Fremont RD, Kihlberg C. Antibiotic use in acute upper respiratory 
tract infections. Am Fam Physician. 2012;86(9):817-822.
practice_questions.reference: Sur DKC, Plesa ML. Antibiotic use in acute upper respiratory tract infections. Am Fam Physician. 2022;106(6):628-636.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["group A beta-hemolytic streptococcal pharyngitis", "GABHS pharyngitis"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": ["Modified Centor Criteria"], "thresholds": ["4 of 5 Centor criteria", "fever 102\u00b0F"], "concept_summary": "Tests understanding that antibiotic treatment for strep pharyngitis prevents rheumatic fever but does not reduce risk of poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis."}

practice_questions.question_text:
A 62-year-old male presents to your office with a 3-month history of increasing dyspnea and cough. 
He has a history of allergic rhinitis and uses fluticasone propionate nasal spray (Flonase) daily. He 
has a 15-pack-year smoking history but quit more than 20 years ago. He has a family history of 
asthma in both his mother and 1 sibling. 
 
Examination findings include a respiratory rate of 18/min and an oxygen saturation of 92% on room 
air. His lung sounds are diminished with scattered rhonchi and no wheezes. The remainder of the 
examination findings are normal. Spirometry shows an FEV /FVC ratio of 0.6 and the FEV  is 70% 
1 1
of predicted with no improvement with a bronchodilator. 
 
Which one of the following is the most likely cause of his dyspnea?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Asthma"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Bronchiectasis"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "COPD"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Heart failure"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Pulmonary fibrosis"}]
practice_questions.explanation:
The most likely cause of this patient’s dyspnea is COPD. This patient has an obstructive 
pattern on spirometry with an FEV /FVC ratio <0.7 and no improvement with 
1
administration of a bronchodilator, which rules out asthma and is consistent with COPD. 
Bronchiectasis is associated with a purulent cough and recurrent lower respiratory tract 
infections on imaging. Heart failure is unlikely because there are no signs of fluid 
overload and this patient’s presentation is more consistent with COPD. Pulmonary 
fibrosis shows a restrictive pattern on spirometry.  
 
References  
Cagle SD Jr, Landrum LS, Kennedy AM. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease: 
diagnosis and management. Am Fam Physician. 2023;107(6):604-612.     
 
Zeller TA, Beben K, Walker S. Distinguishing asthma and COPD in primary care: a case-
based approach. Am Fam Physician. 2023;107(3):247-252.
practice_questions.reference: Gentry S, Gentry B: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease: Diagnosis and management. Am Fam Physician 2017;95(7):433-441.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["COPD", "asthma", "bronchiectasis", "heart failure", "pulmonary fibrosis"], "drugs": ["fluticasone propionate", "bronchodilator"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["FEV1/FVC <0.7", "15-pack-year"], "concept_summary": "Tests spirometry interpretation for COPD diagnosis, specifically that FEV1/FVC <0.7 without bronchodilator response indicates COPD rather than asthma."}

practice_questions.question_text:
A 6-year-old female is brought to the urgent care clinic for evaluation of a cough that has persisted 
for more than 2 months. The cough is dry and nonproductive and seems worse when she goes outside 
in the cold weather. She has not had any recent illnesses. She does not have any wheezing or 
shortness of breath. Her vital signs and an examination are normal. A chest x-ray is unremarkable. 
 
Which one of the following next steps in evaluation should be recommended to determine the most 
likely underlying cause?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Reassurance that no further testing is needed at this time"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Polymerase chain reaction testing for pertussis"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Spirometry"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Bronchoscopy"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "24-hour esophageal pH monitoring"}]
practice_questions.explanation:
This patient most likely has cough variant asthma, a common cause of chronic (lasting 
>8 weeks) cough in children. The minimum appropriate testing for all children with 
chronic cough is chest x-ray and spirometry. Polymerase chain reaction testing for 
pertussis is indicated in children who have had a recent upper respiratory tract illness 
that develops into a classic whooping cough. Bronchoscopy should be considered if 
there is a history of cough that started and persisted after a choking episode, 
suggestive of foreign body aspiration. GERD is another common cause of chronic cough 
but can usually be diagnosed by response to an empiric trial of an antacid medication 
and does not require 24-hour esophageal pH monitoring.  
 
Reference   
Michaudet C, Malaty J. Chronic cough: evaluation and management. Am Fam Physician. 
2017;96(9):575-580.
practice_questions.reference: Michaudet C, Malaty J: Chronic cough: Evaluation and management. Am Fam Physician 2017;96(9):575–580.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["cough variant asthma", "chronic cough"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["chronic cough >8 weeks"], "concept_summary": "Tests recognition that spirometry is required for evaluating chronic cough in children, particularly when cough variant asthma is suspected."}

practice_questions.question_text: A 29-year-old female presents with a long-standing history of episodes of depressed mood, headaches, and irritability that occur in the 1”“2 weeks preceding her menses. She has a history of anxiety that is well controlled on fluoxetine (Prozac), 60 mg daily. She has a copper IUD (Paragard) for contraception. She has tried combined oral contraceptives to control the premenstrual symptoms, but found that they worsened her irritability. Which one of the following treatments would be most effective for this patient's symptoms?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Extended-release bupropion, 150 mg daily"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "Calcium carbonate, 600 mg twice daily"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Dutasteride (Avodart), 0.5 mg daily"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Vitamin D (cholecalciferol), 1000 IU daily 3"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Acupuncture"}]
practice_questions.explanation: Symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS) with mood instability can be reduced with 1000”“1200 mg of calcium carbonate daily (SOR B). Patients with PMS symptoms have been found to have cyclic changes in calcium levels that may account for the symptoms, but the mechanism of action is still unknown. All SSRIs are first-line treatments for symptoms of PMS and premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD), but bupropion has not been shown to be effective. Continuous combined oral contraceptives are also first-line treatment (SOR A). High-dose dutasteride (2.5 mg daily) has been shown to alleviate PMDD symptoms, but it can have a deleterious effect on mood and is teratogenic. Low-dose dutasteride has not demonstrated beneficial effect on PMDD symptoms. Patients with PMS have not been found to have low levels of vitamin D, and supplementing with vitamin D (cholecalciferol) has not been clearly demonstrated to 3 alleviate PMS symptoms. There is insufficient evidence to prove the benefit of acupuncture in reducing PMS symptoms.
practice_questions.reference: Hofmeister S, Bodden S. Premenstrual syndrome and premenstrual dysphoric disorder. Am Fam Physician. 2016;94(3):236-240. | Cary E, Simpson P. Premenstrual disorders and PMDD -a review. Best Pract Res Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2024;38(1):101858.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["premenstrual syndrome", "premenstrual dysphoric disorder"], "drugs": ["calcium carbonate", "fluoxetine", "bupropion", "dutasteride"], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["1000-1200 mg calcium daily"], "concept_summary": "Tests knowledge that calcium supplementation (1000-1200 mg daily) is evidence-based treatment for PMS symptoms when SSRIs are contraindicated or ineffective."}

practice_questions.question_text: An otherwise healthy 15-year-old female presents for evaluation of a 7-month history of heavy menstrual bleeding. Her periods are 10 days long, irregular, and require 10 tampons per day. She sometimes passes large clots. Other than some fatigue, she feels well today and her vital signs are normal. A point-of-care hemoglobin level is 10.2 g/dL (N 12.0”“16.0). Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
practice_questions.choices: [{"letter": "A", "text": "Testing for a bleeding disorder"}, {"letter": "B", "text": "FSH and LH levels"}, {"letter": "C", "text": "Estrogen and progesterone levels"}, {"letter": "D", "text": "Pelvic ultrasonography"}, {"letter": "E", "text": "Pelvic MRI"}]
practice_questions.explanation: Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) is very common in adolescents and may be a sign of an underlying bleeding disorder. Although bleeding disorders affect 1%”“2% of the general population, the prevalence increases to 20% among adolescent girls with heavy menstrual bleeding. Platelet dysfunction and von Willebrand disease are most commonly identified. Evaluation for bleeding disorders is indicated when there is frequent pad or tampon use (>1 every 1”“2 hours); menstrual bleeding lasting longer than 7 days; passage of clots larger than 1 inch in diameter; a personal history of anemia treatment, excessive bleeding with tooth extraction or surgery, or excessive bruising; or a family history of a bleeding disorder. The initial workup for HMB in an adolescent includes a CBC; a prothrombin time, partial thromboplastin time, and INR; a von Willebrand panel; and hCG, ferritin, fibrinogen, and TSH levels. Testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea should be considered in sexually active patients. Testosterone, prolactin, and DHEA levels should be obtained if there are signs of polycystic ovary syndrome or other endocrine disorders. Levels of FSH, LH, estrogen, and progesterone are not helpful in the evaluation of excessive uterine bleeding. Pelvic imaging is not routinely necessary but should be considered for HMB that does not respond to initial management. Hormonal contraceptives and tranexamic acid may be used for the treatment of HMB, but workup for a bleeding disorder is the most important first step.
practice_questions.reference: Oleka C, Dietrich JE. HMB in the adolescent: a review of the modern approach to diagnosis and management. Clin Obstet Gynecol. 2020;63(3):553-560.
practice_questions.concept_tags: {"diagnoses": ["heavy menstrual bleeding", "HMB", "bleeding disorder", "von Willebrand disease"], "drugs": [], "guidelines": [], "thresholds": ["hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL", "20% prevalence in adolescents with HMB", ">1 pad/tampon per 1-2 hours", ">7 days bleeding"], "concept_summary": "Tests recognition that heavy menstrual bleeding in adolescents warrants bleeding disorder evaluation due to 20% prevalence, higher than general population."}
